category_summary:
**OpenAI** dominated headlines with GPT-5.5 [declared their strongest launch ever](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-d14c5d8f5015)—API revenue growing 2x faster than any prior release. Meanwhile, **xAI** [launched Voice Cloning](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-fbdc24149b8e) via API with 80+ voices in 28 languages, drawing massive engagement.

- **François Chollet** reported frontier models still [score below 1% on ARC-AGI-3](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-a67e377903a6), and [warned that RL causes](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-aa20c248aea1) hallucinated performance in unfamiliar domains
- **Ethan Mollick** shared rigorous RCT evidence of [AI therapy improving mental health](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-ba06a65eadb8) by 0.3 SD, plus research showing [o1 outperforming ER doctors](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-b26e162350e6)
- **Elon Musk** [admitted xAI trained Grok](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-ad7005657d89) on OpenAI models, raising major ethical questions about lab competition
- **Clement Delangue** (HuggingFace) [accused labs of 'pulling the ladder'](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-6cf798223e62)—training on open web data then restricting access

A broader theme emerged around AI agents expanding beyond coding into general knowledge work. **swyx** identified ['coding agents breaking containment'](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-abc1787c48e9) as the year's breakout trend, while **Mollick** offered a provocative reframe: [organizations are already superhuman intelligences](/?date=2026-05-02&category=social#item-aa4a51f10ad8), and individual AI productivity tools hit natural limits.

items.title: i said on @jacobeffron's pod recently that "coding agents breaking containment" is the breakout them...
items.content:
i said on @jacobeffron's pod recently that "coding agents breaking containment" is the breakout theme of the year. i meant it - this is the year all knowledge workers, not just coders, get AGI-pilled.

for the AIE EU closing note (https://t.co/P1gILAWvlj), I gave a short talk on how we use agents to run @aidotengineer as a Tiny Team that now serves ~1m unique developers a month for free all around the world, for everything from CMS to renting lobster inflatables.

yes I use @openclaw personally and as a team we use @cognition's Devin and @townai, but this isn't about any one agent; it's about all of them, and how you are probably not trying hard enough to use them for daily knowledge work. 

i hope this gives you agent productivity ideas for you and your team.

items.title: I think the expression is “pulling the ladder”! All labs trained their models by distilling (at the ...
items.content: I think the expression is “pulling the ladder”! All labs trained their models by distilling (at the very least distilling the web) which allowed them to become the fastest growing businesses in the history of humanity and now that they have armies of lawyers and lobbyists, they are trying to prevent others from doing the same thing.

items.title: “Marcus’ specific point about coding is structurally important: a model that produces code which com...
items.content:
“Marcus’ specific point about coding is structurally important: a model that produces code which compiles and passes the tests it was given is not the same as a model that produces correct, secure, maintainable, well-architected software. The first is verifiable in seconds; the second requires the kind of judgement that has been the historical bottleneck on engineering productivity.

[OpenAI President Greg]  Brockman acknowledges the gap, even as he argues it is closing. “The technology we have right now is very jagged,” he said in the Big Technology interview. “It is absolutely superhuman at many tasks. When it comes to writing code, those kinds of things, the AI can just do it. But there’s some very basic tasks that a human can do that our AI still struggles with.”
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Marcus quotes discussion about AI coding quality - distinguishing between code that compiles/passes tests vs correct, secure, maintainable software. Includes Brockman acknowledging the gap.
items.reasoning: Important nuanced discussion about real limitations of AI coding from both critic and OpenAI President. Addresses key practical concern about AI-generated code quality.
items.summary_html: Marcus quotes discussion about AI coding quality - distinguishing between code that compiles/passes tests vs correct, secure, maintainable software. Includes Brockman acknowledging the gap.
items.content_html:
“Marcus’ specific point about coding is structurally important: a model that produces code which compiles and passes the tests it was given is not the same as a model that produces correct, secure, maintainable, well-architected software. [OpenAI President Greg] Brockman acknowledges the gap, even as he argues it is closing. “The technology we have right now is very jagged,” he said in the Big Technology interview. “It is absolutely superhuman at many tasks. When it comes to writing code, those kinds of things, the AI can just do it. But there’s some very basic tasks that a human can do that our AI still struggles with.”

items.title:
Researchers just figured out why looped transformers are so powerful.

Most language models store hu...
items.content:
Researchers just figured out why looped transformers are so powerful.

Most language models store huge amounts of knowledge but fail to combine facts in a single pass.

Ask one a 10-step reasoning question and it breaks.

A new paper explores looped transformers, an architecture that runs the same layers over and over inside one forward pass.

Each loop deepens the reasoning without adding parameters.

Trained from scratch on multi-hop tasks, the looped version solves problems standard transformers cannot touch.

It generalizes to fact chains never combined during training.

Even better, a model trained on 20-hop questions answers 30-hop questions at inference.

The trick is simply running more loops at test time.

This ability emerges through a sharp three-stage shift:

> Memorizing training examples first
> Generalizing within known patterns
> Composing fully unseen knowledge

One catch surfaced.

Too many loops triggers overthinking and answers degrade.

Code, checkpoints, and data are open-source.

items.summary: AlphaSignalAI explains a new paper on looped transformers - architecture that runs same layers repeatedly in one forward pass, enabling multi-hop reasoning. Trained on 20-hop questions, generalizes to 30-hop. Three-stage learning shift discovered. Overthinking failure mode identified.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical content about a novel architecture for improving transformer reasoning. Looped transformers as an alternative to scaling parameters is an important research direction. Open-source. Good detail level.

items.content:
The Sierra research team has contributed many of the leading industry benchmarks for agents grounded in our ambition to have the most robust and sophisticated agents in customer experience. 𝜏-voice is our new benchmark for real-time voice agents.

𝜏-voice measures 278 grounded customer service tasks across retail, airline, and telecom. It pairs deterministic, end-to-end task scoring with realistic, controllable audio across diverse personas, environmental noise, and free-form turn-taking. https://t.co/U7hMf6FNEX

items.title: In this article https://t.co/IczpakP3s4 the authors apply the following methodology to estimate the ...
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov shares a paper methodology for estimating closed-source LLM parameter counts using factual knowledge benchmarks and log-linear regression from open-weight models. Provides estimated parameter counts for frontier models.
items.reasoning: Technically interesting research that reveals hidden information about closed models. Good engagement. Novel methodology with practical implications for understanding the competitive landscape.
items.summary_html: Burkov shares a paper methodology for estimating closed-source LLM parameter counts using factual knowledge benchmarks and log-linear regression from open-weight models. Provides estimated parameter counts for frontier models.
items.content_html:
In this article https://t.co/IczpakP3s4 the authors apply the following methodology to estimate the number of parameters in the frontier closed source LLMs:

(1) build a dataset of 1,400 factual questions spanning 7 tiers of obscurity, deliberately picking facts that can't be derived by reasoning or compressed away by smarter architectures;

(2) evaluate 89 open-weight models with known parameter counts (135M–1.6T params, 19 vendors) on this benchmark;

(3) fit a log-linear regression between IKP accuracy and log(parameters);

(4) plug closed-source frontier models into that calibration curve to back out their parameter counts.

The intuition is an information-theoretic lower bound: storing F facts requires at least F/(bits per parameter) weights, so what a model knows lower-bounds how many parameters it has.

The estimated numbers of parameters for some models are shown in the below table in the "Estimate w/ corrections" table.

items.title:
You can now run an AI agent on-device, right inside Chrome. 

No servers. No data leaving your devic...
items.content:
You can now run an AI agent on-device, right inside Chrome. 

No servers. No data leaving your device.

The browser extension uses Gemma 4 E2B, a 2B parameter model with native tool calling baked into its chat template.

The model decides when to call tools and which ones, all accelerated by WebGPU on your machine.

Here is what it can actually do:

> Search browsing history by meaning
> Read and summarize any page
> Open, close, switch tabs
> Highlight elements on screen

The architecture is the clever part.

A background script loads the weights once and shares them across every tab.

A side panel keeps your chat alive while you browse.

Content scripts handle DOM access for retrieval and highlighting.

Page content gets embedded with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and stored in IndexedDB for semantic search.

It is open-source and built on Transformers.js inside Manifest V3.

items.reasoning: Technically detailed description of on-device AI agent in the browser using Google's Gemma 4 model. Demonstrates practical edge AI with no server dependency. Good architectural details. Relevant to on-device AI and browser agent trends.

The architecture is the clever part.

A background script loads the weights once and shares them across every tab.

A side panel keeps your chat alive while you browse.

Content scripts handle DOM access for retrieval and highlighting.

Page content gets embedded with all-MiniLM-L6-v2 and stored in IndexedDB for semantic search.

It is open-source and built on Transformers.js inside Manifest V3.

items.title:
And.....✅ DONE!

The Vibe Jam of 2026 sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif + @tripoai is...
items.content:
And.....✅ DONE!

The Vibe Jam of 2026 sponsored by @cursor_ai + @boltdotnew + @heyglif + @tripoai is officially closed

🕹️ 945 games submitted
🛝 242,212 players
👁️ ~12 million views on X

Now me and @s13k_ will start the judging process, probably pre-vetting games first with some help from AI (like if the games load at all) and then they go on to all the judges

I want to thank everyone who participated! ❤️ There's only 3 cash prizes but even if you don't win, I hope you all had fun creating things, which is the best part of AI for me, it lets me create things I could never have dreamt of making before

It's already clear to me from the submissions that AI's ability to help you create beautiful and fun games has progressed a lot, last year's games looked clunky and basic, this year's games are starting to look like stuff you could find on Steam

There's no specific deadline for when judging is done but we'll try to be as fast as possible, last year it took 2 weeks I think!

THANK YOU!!!

If you think you are going to get alignment out of LLMs you are sadly mistaken.

If you live in a society in which people are rolling out LLMs at massive scale, without a robust solution to alignment (or even managing gremlins) you’re probably fucked.

Resist the proliferation of LLMs, before it’s too late.

items.title: The fact that the context of LLMs, especially in the agentic setting, is still stored as raw text in...
items.content:
The fact that the context of LLMs, especially in the agentic setting, is still stored as raw text instead of a continuously updated embedding tensor surprises me.

People invent crazy tricks to paraphrase or regex-replace the long context into a shorter text, or to use images to contain the context's text and then OCR these images.

A scientific solution must be embedding-based, and the neural network must learn end-to-end to embed the context of any length by learning a gating mechanism that decides what the embedding tensor must absorb and what it should ignore.

Text must only be needed in the output because code is text and the user can read text. The model must think and remember in embeddings.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues LLM context should be stored as continuously updated embedding tensors rather than raw text, with a learned gating mechanism. Text should only be needed for output. The model should think and remember in embeddings rather than text.
items.reasoning: Substantive technical insight about LLM architecture limitations with good engagement (141 likes, 11K views). Proposes a specific architectural direction that addresses real scalability issues with current context windows.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues LLM context should be stored as continuously updated embedding tensors rather than raw text, with a learned gating mechanism. Text should only be needed for output. The model should think and remember in embeddings rather than text.

items.title: Interim CEO Peter Clark shares his thoughts on this moment for Ai2, our commitment to open science, ...
items.content:
Interim CEO Peter Clark shares his thoughts on this moment for Ai2, our commitment to open science, and where the institute is headed next. 👇

You've been part of Ai2 for many years, including as Interim CEO before. What feels different about this moment?

What feels different now is the incredible pace of progress in AI—and the risk that, in that acceleration, we lose sight of some of the longer-term work that really matters.

Ai2 was created to take that longer-horizon view. From the beginning, Paul Allen's vision was to advance AI in ways that push science forward while also delivering meaningful benefit to the world, and, critically, doing it in the open. That's a commitment that's become even more important in the current landscape.

So for me, stepping back into this role is about renewing our commitment to that mission, and maintaining a sharpened focus on long-term, high-impact research and engineering. There's a real sense of momentum here internally. Teams are coming together, new ideas are emerging, and there's a lot of energy around what comes next.

Where has that mission shown up in the projects at Ai2?

It’s always been a combination of deep research and real-world impact. Early on, we helped set the stage for the LLM revolution with our project ELMo, and more recently, that work turned into Olmo and Molmo, along with new training approaches like FlexOlmo. All these projects show that you can be open and still operate at the frontier.

At the same time, we’ve been applying AI in meaningful ways. Systems like AutoDiscovery are already changing how oncologists think about treatments for certain types of cancer, and efforts like OlmoEarth are helping us better understand complex Earth systems. That connection between advancing the science of AI and actually deploying these tools is really central to how we operate.

Where do you think Ai2 is doing something meaningfully different?

A lot of the work we focus on doesn’t fit neatly into existing incentives. Some of it is just longer-term. It’s more exploratory, where the path to impact is real but not immediate. And some of it is about openness and building in a way that others can actually understand and expand on.

As a nonprofit, we have the flexibility to do that. We can spend time on problems that need sustained attention, even if they’re not tied to short-term outcomes.

It also shapes the kind of environment we’re trying to create. People have the space to think long-term, to explore ideas that aren’t fully formed yet, and to work in the open—while still aiming toward something that has real impact.

How do you think about the role of open models in Ai2’s work?

Open models remain fundamental to what we do. They matter not just because they improve access, but because they enable a deeper scientific understanding of how these systems work. When models are open, the broader community can study them, build on them, and push the field forward more quickly.

That’s also a big part of what’s driving our NSF OMAI work. The U.S. National Science Foundation and NVIDIA invested in us to create the next generation of open model development by bringing together the compute, infrastructure, and research needed to build systems that are fully open and transparent.

We’re excited to share an update on the OMAI project very soon as our teams get to work developing the next generation of models and foundational research that will come from it.

Where is the Institute planning to focus its time and energy going forward?

There are a few areas we’re leaning into.

One is continuing to advance the science of AI systems, especially around understanding how models behave and how to make them more reliable. There’s still a lot we don’t fully understand there, and industry-wide, too much of that work is happening behind closed doors. The NSF OMAI project I mentioned earlier is a big part of this.

Another is AI for scientific research and discovery. We’re building Asta, an agentic ecosystem that can help researchers generate hypotheses, connect ideas, and move faster. Tools within Asta such as ScholarQA, AutoDiscovery, and Theorizer exemplify this direction, helping scientists by analyzing the scientific literature, discovering surprising findings in data, and positing explanatory theories.

Something we’re also really excited to keep advancing is embodied AI. There’s a lot of interesting work happening at the intersection of language models and physical systems, and some of our early efforts like MolmoAct and MolmoBot are starting to explore what it means to build more general, adaptable systems that can operate in the real world, and we have a lot more to come this year.

And then there’s our AI for the planet work—including the environment, conservation, and global systems. These are areas where our work is already having real, long-term impact, and we see huge potential for AI to help those on the ground make an even greater impact as platforms like OlmoEarth grow and expand.

How does all of this translate to real-world impact?

We’ve always tried to connect the research to something that can actually be used. In practice, that means moving across a spectrum from fundamental research to early prototypes to systems with real-world applications. And often, the interesting part is how those pieces connect.

AutoDiscovery is a good example. It began as a research system for automated, open-ended scientific discovery and is now available as a managed solution where researchers can upload structured datasets, generate and test hypotheses, and inspect the code and statistical analysis behind each result.

It’s not a linear path, but the progression from exploration to application is where a lot of the impact actually happens.

Looking ahead—what kind of future is Ai2 working to build, and how can people be part of it?

We’re working toward a future where AI is both more deeply understood and more broadly useful.

That means continuing to make systems more transparent and reliable, while also applying those advances in ways that have real impact – whether that’s accelerating scientific discovery or addressing challenges in areas like the environment and global systems.

The opportunity is to do both at once, and to do it in the open. And that really resonates with a certain kind of person: those who want to work on ambitious problems, who are motivated by impact, and who value being part of a broader scientific effort.

That’s the kind of community we’re continuing to build.

items.title:
AI and GDP - what does it mean?

Sure, AI is “contributing” to GDP  — but @davidsacks really nails i...
items.content:
AI and GDP - what does it mean?

Sure, AI is “contributing” to GDP  — but @davidsacks really nails it below, “Paying people to dig holes and fill them back up increases GDP, as it is currently measured, even though that activity has no value.”

In the case of AI, GDP is going up because of *investments* in AI infrastructure, but not because users in general are getting Return on Investment (most haven’t, so far, per multiple studies).

The future of our nation doesn’t rest on building data centers; it rests on building AI that is *good enough to consistently add value for its customers*.  

If it doesn’t, all the building of data centers will turn out to have been an enormous black hole, temporarily good for GDP but of little lasting value.
items.author: @GaryMarcus
items.summary: Gary Marcus analyzes AI and GDP, arguing investment in AI infrastructure inflates GDP without corresponding ROI for users, citing Sacks that spending alone doesn't equal value creation
items.reasoning: Substantive economic argument about AI value creation vs investment. Important counternarrative to AI hype. Cites specific economic analysis.

items.title:
I love how much we can "teach" Claude by giving it skills and access to new MCP servers.

A few thin...
items.content:
I love how much we can "teach" Claude by giving it skills and access to new MCP servers.

A few things you can now do with Pika MCP:

• Drop in a GitHub repo URL and get a walkthrough video with a lip-synced presenter in the corner and captions

• Give it a project link and get a fully edited launch film with title cards, B-roll, and beat-synced generated music

• Hand it a topic and get a podcast-style talking-head video, captioned

There are 26 skills here focused on generation, editing, and analysis. You can access all of them directly from Claude.

items.title:
A Claude Code session becomes unusable at around 60% context.

Auto-compaction kicks in a little bit...
items.content:
A Claude Code session becomes unusable at around 60% context.

Auto-compaction kicks in a little bit over 80%, and that's when most people notice what's going on.

I don't let my context get past 50%.

Run /statusline in Claude Code, and it will always display how much context you are using.

Once I get to 50%, I dump the entire context to a file and /clear it.
items.author: @svpino
items.summary: Svpino shares Claude Code context management strategy: sessions become unusable at 60% context, auto-compaction at 80%. Recommends dumping context to file and clearing at 50%. Shares /statusline command tip.
items.reasoning: Highly practical advice for Claude Code users with specific thresholds. Very high engagement (36k views, 359 likes). Actionable developer workflow tip.
items.summary_html: Svpino shares Claude Code context management strategy: sessions become unusable at 60% context, auto-compaction at 80%. Recommends dumping context to file and clearing at 50%. Shares /statusline command tip.
items.content_html:
A Claude Code session becomes unusable at around 60% context.

Auto-compaction kicks in a little bit over 80%, and that's when most people notice what's going on.

I don't let my context get past 50%.

Run /statusline in Claude Code, and it will always display how much context you are using.

Once I get to 50%, I dump the entire context to a file and /clear it.

items.title:
What if chat is the wrong interface for managing agents?

In this talk, @steveruizok  shows what cha...
items.content:
What if chat is the wrong interface for managing agents?

In this talk, @steveruizok  shows what changes when agents move onto a shared canvas instead of staying trapped in a linear thread. Using tldraw's Fairydraw experiment, where users collaborated with three "fairies" coordinating with both humans and each other, this is a practical look at what spatial interfaces make possible for agent workflows.

https://t.co/IQqqGHY7NY

items.title:
Code reviews will benefit a ton from this.

I've been familiar with Macroscope for a while. They han...
items.content:
Code reviews will benefit a ton from this.

I've been familiar with Macroscope for a while. They handle the basics:

• Identifying bugs
• Security flaws
• Runtime issues

They just released Check Run Agents. I think this will make a ton of impact.

I watched their video, and here are a few examples they gave:

• They have a Principles agent that enforces coding conventions and catches things like mangled primary keys.

• An Architecture Diagram agent that draws a visual of what changed in a PR and posts it to Slack for human reviewers.

• Agents for unit tests and database migration checklists.

You can configure each agent's model, reasoning effort, file path scoping, custom prompt, and tool selection.

You can run these agents on every pull request as native GitHub check runs. Define what to check in a markdown file, connect your tools, and let the agents run automatically.

Honestly, we are living in a time where we can use every single tool that helps us build better software.

You can run these agents on every pull request as native GitHub check runs. Define what to check in a markdown file, connect your tools, and let the agents run automatically.

Honestly, we are living in a time where we can use every single tool that helps us build better software.

items.title: San Francisco needs culture, @HugoAmsellem writes. Love how he puts it “culture gives technology mea...
items.content:
San Francisco needs culture, @HugoAmsellem writes. Love how he puts it “culture gives technology meaning.”

Yesterday I ran a panel with @PSYONICinc’s founder at Silicon Valley AI Hub. 

@DOUBLExA He makes robotic hands for both robots and humans who need a new hand. Here is a photo of him using one to hold the microphone.

Thought of him when reading this post. His stories about how technology helps humans hit me hard. 

Technology can dramatically improve human life. We need to tell those stories better. Me included.

The only rule is that your build has to feature a large number between 1 and 10. Check the replies in this thread for sample projects to draw inspiration from or remix.

You can find more info and directions on how to submit your builds here: https://t.co/CusPvfKIC3

items.title: Attribution—figuring out which input features are responsible for a neural network's output, such as...
items.content:
Attribution—figuring out which input features are responsible for a neural network's output, such as which pixels in an image led to its classification—is a problem with no obvious right answer, and the field had accumulated several competing methods that often disagreed on the same input.

Rather than judging methods by how convincing their visualizations look, the authors of this ICML 2017 paper write down two properties any reasonable attribution method should have, then demonstrate that most existing techniques (raw gradients, DeepLift, LRP, guided backpropagation, deconvolution) violate at least one.

The first property says that a feature whose change actually moves the prediction should not receive zero credit; the second says that two networks computing the same function should produce identical attributions regardless of how they are internally wired.

Their proposed method, Integrated Gradients, averages the gradient along a straight line from a chosen reference input (a black image, for vision models) to the actual input, and this small construction satisfies both properties while requiring no modifications to the model—just repeated calls to the standard gradient operator used in backpropagation.

They also link their straight-line choice to a result from cost-sharing in game theory, which singles it out among possible paths as the unique one that treats symmetric features identically.

Both the method and the framing have aged well: Integrated Gradients still appears in interpretability toolkits nearly a decade later, and the broader move of using a few explicit axioms to cut through a debate that empirical comparison alone could not settle is a useful template for other machine learning problems where ground truth is unclear.

ChapterPal: https://t.co/Atnewi8WnW

PDF: https://t.co/qOjI9d43Mj

items.title:
✨ I just replaced Mapbox on all my sites with OpenFreeMap by @hyperknot and my map bill is now $0

M...
items.content:
✨ I just replaced Mapbox on all my sites with OpenFreeMap by @hyperknot and my map bill is now $0

Mapbox's pricing is getting increasingly extortionary (which is fine, it's capitalism) but at some point you have to think, $857/month for what? A map? Really? A map is that expensive? How can loading a map be that expensive? It's just some PNG tiles you host somewhere? Why?

@OpenFreeMapOrg is 100% free and all you do is point your AI to openfreemap(dot)org and tell it to replace Mapbox with that

5 minutes and you save thousands $$$ per year!

Apparently @Cloudflare sponsors its bandwidth which is very cool and keeps it online!

items.title:
What signals that a company is getting AI transformation right?

I asked Tracie Larson, VP and GM of...
items.content:
What signals that a company is getting AI transformation right?

I asked Tracie Larson, VP and GM of Customer Success, US Financial Services of @Microsoft  at #MicrosoftAITour Atlanta. 

Her answer: 
✅ Executive sponsorship with intent 
✅ Governance built in from day one 
✅ AI fluency at every level 
✅ An AI center of excellence
I'd add a fifth: customer-back design.

As Pam Maynard OBE said: "The most important ingredients in any AI solution are intelligence and trust."
Frontier firms amplify human ambition. That's the standard.

▶ Watch my full conversation with Tracie: https://t.co/rrKIeWCOXP
▶ Read the Microsoft AI Powered Sales eBook: https://t.co/Cohu3treVy

#FrontierFirm #AITransformation #MicrosoftAITour #MicrosoftAmbassodor #FinancialServices #CROs

items.summary: Helen Yu shares insights from Microsoft AI Tour Atlanta about signals of successful AI transformation: executive sponsorship, governance, AI fluency, center of excellence, and customer-back design
items.reasoning: Enterprise AI transformation framework from Microsoft event. Somewhat promotional/corporate but provides structured thinking about AI adoption signals.
items.summary_html: Helen Yu shares insights from Microsoft AI Tour Atlanta about signals of successful AI transformation: executive sponsorship, governance, AI fluency, center of excellence, and customer-back design
items.content_html:
What signals that a company is getting AI transformation right?

I asked Tracie Larson, VP and GM of Customer Success, US Financial Services of @Microsoft at #MicrosoftAITour Atlanta.

Her answer:

✅ Executive sponsorship with intent

✅ Governance built in from day one

✅ AI fluency at every level

✅ An AI center of excellence

I'd add a fifth: customer-back design.

As Pam Maynard OBE said: "The most important ingredients in any AI solution are intelligence and trust."

Frontier firms amplify human ambition. That's the standard.

▶ Watch my full conversation with Tracie: https://t.co/rrKIeWCOXP

▶ Read the Microsoft AI Powered Sales eBook: https://t.co/Cohu3treVy

#FrontierFirm #AITransformation #MicrosoftAITour #MicrosoftAmbassodor #FinancialServices #CROs

Sometimes partial presence is better than none.

items.title:
@iBrews I have talked to some friends at Apple and same. I don’t get where this “leak” came from.

T...
items.content:
@iBrews I have talked to some friends at Apple and same. I don’t get where this “leak” came from.

That said maybe it is just that glasses are in their way and that isn’t seen as a Vision Pro. 

So much bullshit out there about it.

People don’t know how good the glasses are. That said I expect the first ones to be audio and cameras with no displays. Which confuses things a bit.

Still waiting for the future.

items.title: Hey @DHLCanadaHelp, your customer service really and truly sucks. You claimed to try to deliver a pa...
items.content:
Hey @DHLCanadaHelp, your customer service really and truly sucks. You claimed to try to deliver a package, but didn’t actually contact me, didn’t leave a service card, your automated software won’t let me change delivery options, and I literally cannot reach a human. 

You have simply held an important package without delivering it or giving me any recourse. You might as well have stolen it.

F-. 

Second time in two weeks, this time worse than first.

items.title:
@naasking > When it's text, hallucinations are apparent. You can just look.

And what's the interest...
items.content:
@naasking > When it's text, hallucinations are apparent. You can just look.

And what's the interest in storing hallucinations? All that  an LLM prints is a hallucination and nothing of what it printed before or after calling a tool can be used to explain the tool call. No generated token has a meaningful or provable connection to any other token.
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov argues all LLM-generated text is hallucination and no generated token has a meaningful or provable connection to any other token, making audit of text before/after tool calls meaningless.
items.reasoning: Provocative technical claim about LLM behavior, but expressed in a reply thread with minimal engagement.
items.summary_html: Burkov argues all LLM-generated text is hallucination and no generated token has a meaningful or provable connection to any other token, making audit of text before/after tool calls meaningless.
items.content_html:
@naasking > When it's text, hallucinations are apparent. You can just look.

And what's the interest in storing hallucinations? All that an LLM prints is a hallucination and nothing of what it printed before or after calling a tool can be used to explain the tool call. No generated token has a meaningful or provable connection to any other token.

items.title: This NeurIPS 2018 Spotlight paper introduces a simple, architecture-agnostic Mahalanobis distance-ba...
items.content:
This NeurIPS 2018 Spotlight paper introduces a simple, architecture-agnostic Mahalanobis distance-based method that uniquely achieves state-of-the-art performance in simultaneously detecting both out-of-distribution samples and adversarial attacks, even under challenging conditions like noisy labels or limited data, and enables class-incremental learning without retraining.

Learn with ChapterPal: https://t.co/P1ZbveLjne

PDF: https://t.co/zFPWlJH3WG

items.title:
The Framework 13 Pro is truly in another league.

I have a pre-production version (the one I bought ...
items.content:
The Framework 13 Pro is truly in another league.

I have a pre-production version (the one I bought gets here in July), and this thing is beautiful.

I've been using this for an hour, so I'll post a more detailed review in a few days, but for the first time ever I feel this can go toe to toe with a MacBook in terms of quality.

As a reference, I've been a Mac user since 2009. 

Fast. Top-notch trackpad and keyboard. 4:3 touchscreen display. Upgradeable. Infinite battery life.

Here is a picture of me writing this tweet. I'll record a video next week.

items.title:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the ML monitoring and observability interview

Thi...
items.content:
A new curriculum on @ChapterPal: Prep reading for the ML monitoring and observability interview

This curriculum offers a comprehensive technical foundation for monitoring, evaluating, and maintaining production-level machine learning systems.

It begins by framing the challenges of technical debt and system reliability before progressing to specialized strategies for data validation, the mitigation of concept drift, and the identification of distribution shifts.

The syllabus further explores deep learning through the lens of robust uncertainty estimation, out-of-distribution detection, and anomaly identification, ensuring learners can distinguish between reliable model outputs and unexpected edge cases.

The curriculum concludes with advanced methods for model interpretability and feature attribution, providing the diagnostic tools necessary to audit model logic, build stakeholder trust, and fulfill essential production readiness standards.

https://t.co/QKZdLOM0NK

Use the Subscribe button to receive email updates to this curriculum.

items.title: New feature on @ChapterPal: the reader can now add a quiz on demand and choose what content it shoul...
items.content:
New feature on @ChapterPal: the reader can now add a quiz on demand and choose what content it should cover.

Previously, quizzes could only be created for the entire chapter by asking a multi-agent system to identify the topic to test and the places to put the quizzes.

Some users wanted more quizzes and more granular ones. Now the user can test themselves as often as they feel as they progress in the reading.

ChapterPal is without a doubt the best app to learn from scientific literature. AI is there to unblock you when you are stuck and to challenge you to test your understanding.

The *active reading* approach, where the reader decides when to reveal the content and when to ask questions, blurred terms that prevent superficial skimming, highlights and note-taking, all this provably contributes to better retention.

New feature on @ChapterPal: the reader can now add a quiz on demand and choose what content it should cover.

Previously, quizzes could only be created for the entire chapter by asking a multi-agent system to identify the topic to test and the places to put the quizzes.

Some users wanted more quizzes and more granular ones. Now the user can test themselves as often as they feel as they progress in the reading.

ChapterPal is without a doubt the best app to learn from scientific literature. AI is there to unblock you when you are stuck and to challenge you to test your understanding.

The *active reading* approach, where the reader decides when to reveal the content and when to ask questions, blurred terms that prevent superficial skimming, highlights and note-taking, all this provably contributes to better retention.

items.title: When I create a learning curriculum on @ChapterPal, some papers I decide to put on the curriculum do...
items.content:
When I create a learning curriculum on @ChapterPal, some papers I decide to put on the curriculum don't have a permissive Creative Commons license. When I know that the copyright belongs to the authors, I contact the authors and ask whether they are OK with me hosting their paper on ChapterPal.

Most of the time, I get no response. Then when I do get a response, 9 out of 10 are positive, but there's this tenth answer that drives me crazy.

The answer usually is just "No."

First of all, why? I mean, what's the harm? But most importantly, and this is the part that drives me crazy is why the hell have they decided to answer "No" instead of just ignoring the request?

Am I overthinking, or do these people have some serious issues?
items.author: @burkov
items.summary: Burkov discusses frustration with authors who refuse permission to host their papers on ChapterPal, questioning why someone would respond 'No' rather than just ignoring the request.
items.reasoning: Tangentially relevant to AI education and open access but mostly a personal grievance.
items.summary_html: Burkov discusses frustration with authors who refuse permission to host their papers on ChapterPal, questioning why someone would respond 'No' rather than just ignoring the request.
items.content_html:
When I create a learning curriculum on @ChapterPal, some papers I decide to put on the curriculum don't have a permissive Creative Commons license. When I know that the copyright belongs to the authors, I contact the authors and ask whether they are OK with me hosting their paper on ChapterPal.

Most of the time, I get no response. Then when I do get a response, 9 out of 10 are positive, but there's this tenth answer that drives me crazy.

The answer usually is just "No."

First of all, why? I mean, what's the harm? But most importantly, and this is the part that drives me crazy is why the hell have they decided to answer "No" instead of just ignoring the request?

Am I overthinking, or do these people have some serious issues?

Commvault provides comprehensive ransomware cloud recovery by combining air-gapped backups, early threat detection, and automated orchestration to restore data to AWS, Azure, or Google Cloud in minutes.

Here are some key features of #Commvault's AI-enhanced Fast, Clean Cloud Recovery and preemptive Resilience solutions:
🟣Early Threat Detection
🟣Isolated Recovery (Cleanroom)
🟣Rapid Automated Recovery

🟣Cloud-Native Protection
🟣Multi-Cloud

items.title: @BrianHJacobson This mindset is unfortunately too prevalent. I believe it’s one of the main reasons ...
items.content: @BrianHJacobson This mindset is unfortunately too prevalent. I believe it’s one of the main reasons why social capital has completely evaporated in this country. I moved into a pretty dense walkable neighborhood *exactly* in order to counter it. I don’t always see eye to eye with my neighbors, but that’s exactly the point. Being able to interact and get along with people that you are not really friends with on a regular basis is really, really healthy, both for individuals and for the society at large.

items.title:
I still haven't solved the CO2 bedroom challenge

You open the window and you wake up from a 6am gar...
items.content:
I still haven't solved the CO2 bedroom challenge

You open the window and you wake up from a 6am garbage truck or barking dogs and sunlight

You close it, you suffocate in 1200 ppl at 5am

I guess you really need some mini tube in your wall with a vent that opens and closed based on internal CO2 but how do I build that?

items.title:
Where will the future of AI-led business be built?

Last year at #PegaWorld I caught up with @Pega's...
items.content:
Where will the future of AI-led business be built?

Last year at #PegaWorld I caught up with @Pega's CPO @pegakerim right after his keynote. This year I'm back and hosting two special guests on #CXOSpice.

The lesson that resonates: AI has two jobs.
• Design time → be creative, prototype fast
• Run time → be deterministic, auditable, predictable
Most orgs miss that line. That's the difference between AI hype and AI that works.

@pegakerim is back on the keynote stage at #PegaWorld 2026 (June 7–9, Las Vegas) with leaders from MetLife, Wells Fargo, Unum, AWS & Cognizant.
100K sq ft of live demos. 200+ hands-on experiences. Full satisfaction guarantee; attend 2 days or your money back + $2K travel.

$200 off until May 1 → code TRANSFORM26
Registration: https://t.co/Sv50vDX868

PegaWorld is where Agentic AI gets real. Let’s experience it together.

#AgenticAI #EnterpriseAI #AI #PegaWorld #PegaPartner
